skill.name: github-project-management
skill.description: Manage GitHub issues, project boards, sprints, and swarm-coordinated work using GitHub CLI, Claude Flow, and ruv-swarm workflows.
skill.summary: Coordinate GitHub project work with issue triage, board automation, sprint planning, and swarm-based task tracking.
skill.author: Claude Code

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many external command, network, filesystem, and weak crypto patterns, but most are Markdown examples for GitHub CLI, ruv-swarm, Claude Flow, project URLs, and templates. The high weak cryptography alerts are false positives; no prompt injection, credential theft, obfuscation, or unauthorized exfiltration was found. Risk remains medium because following the guide runs third-party CLIs and automation that can modify GitHub issues, projects, webhooks, and reports.

medium_findings.title: State-Changing GitHub Command Automation
medium_findings.description: The skill documents GitHub CLI and ruv-swarm commands that create issues, edit labels, close stale issues, update project cards, and post comments. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but unsafe execution could alter repository project data.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited examples clearly run state-changing GitHub and swarm commands. The behavior matches the skill purpose, so this is operational risk rather than malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Unpinned Third-Party Tool Execution
medium_findings.description: The examples invoke third-party automation through npx and a GitHub Action tag. These dependencies are not pinned to immutable digests, which creates supply-chain risk if users execute the examples without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file shows external package and action execution in workflow examples. Legitimate use is possible, but the lack of immutable pinning is a real supply-chain concern.

medium_findings.title: External Synchronization and Reporting Destinations
medium_findings.description: The skill includes examples for webhook synchronization, Slack or email report distribution, cross-organization synchronization, and GitHub project URLs. These are expected features, but they can expose project metadata if configured incorrectly.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The destinations are visible in operational examples and are not hidden. Risk depends on user configuration and repository sensitivity.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The high-severity weak crypto alerts point to ordinary descriptions, template labels, and project-management wording. No cryptographic implementation, hash comparison, cipher selection, or password handling was found in those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines found documentation and issue-template text, not crypto APIs or algorithms. The static pattern matched unrelated words.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Device Access Is Benign Error Redirection
low_findings.description: The filesystem alert is standard stderr redirection while removing a stale label. It does not read local files, write sensitive data, or access arbitrary paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line only suppresses an expected command error. This is a common shell pattern and not evidence of local filesystem abuse.

low_findings.title: Documentation URL Alerts Are Mostly Benign
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URLs are primarily documentation links and GitHub issue URLs used in examples. The webhook placeholder needs user review, but there is no evidence of a covert or suspicious endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible, contextual, and related to GitHub or project documentation. No suspicious domain or secret-bearing request was found.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Alerts Are Project Metrics Context
low_findings.description: The system and network reconnaissance alerts map to project IDs, backup IDs, milestone tracking, and metrics language. These are normal project-management concepts, not host or network discovery commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are part of GitHub project configuration or analytics text. I did not find host scanning, port scanning, or network enumeration intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Substitution With Repository Data
dangerous_patterns.description: Several examples capture GitHub issue, project, and generated task data through shell command substitution. This is useful for automation, but it can propagate untrusted issue content into later commands if copied without safeguards.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command substitutions are explicit and process repository content. The examples often quote variables, so the concern is misuse risk rather than confirmed injection.

dangerous_patterns.title: Automated Stale Issue Closure
dangerous_patterns.description: The stale issue workflow can comment on, label, and close issues based on computed age and swarm analysis. This can cause unwanted project changes if run with broad permissions or weak review controls.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The example includes direct issue edit and close operations. The intent is legitimate maintenance, but the impact is real if executed incorrectly.

dangerous_patterns.title: Webhook and Cross-Organization Sync Configuration
dangerous_patterns.description: The guide includes webhook and cross-organization synchronization examples. These patterns require explicit approval because they can move project metadata outside the current repository or organization.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples are clear and user-facing rather than covert. They still deserve warning because they cross repository or organization boundaries.

content.user_title: Manage GitHub Projects With Swarm Coordination
content.value_statement: Teams lose time when issues, project boards, and sprint status drift apart. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through coordinated GitHub project workflows.
actual_capabilities: Create and organize GitHub issues with labels, assignees, and swarm-ready metadata.
Convert issues into coordinated swarm tasks and subtasks for implementation planning.
Synchronize GitHub project boards with issue status, priority, and agent workload fields.
Plan sprints, milestones, releases, dependencies, and cross-repository coordination workflows.
Generate project metrics for cycle time, throughput, burndown, and team workload.
Provide reusable issue templates for integration tasks, bug reports, feature requests, and swarm tasks.
limitations: Requires GitHub CLI authentication and repository permissions before commands can work.
Assumes ruv-swarm or Claude Flow tooling is installed and configured separately.
Command examples can change live GitHub data and should be reviewed before execution.
Does not replace GitHub access controls, audit logs, or human approval for sensitive projects.

use_cases.title: Coordinate Feature Delivery
use_cases.description: Break a feature issue into swarm-managed tasks, labels, milestones, and project board updates.

use_cases.title: Improve Issue Triage
use_cases.description: Classify new and stale issues, suggest labels, identify duplicates, and keep backlog status current.

use_cases.title: Track Sprint Health
use_cases.description: Use board analytics, burndown reports, and workload views to monitor delivery progress.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Swarm-Ready Issue
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for one feature request.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the GitHub project management skill to draft a swarm-ready issue for this feature. Include title, labels, acceptance criteria, and a project board placement plan.

prompt_templates.title: Triage an Issue Backlog
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate review of existing open issues.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the open GitHub issues for this repository. Suggest labels, priorities, duplicate links, stale issue actions, and items that need human review before changes are made.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Sprint Board
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced sprint and project board organization.
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a sprint planning workflow for this GitHub project. Include milestones, board columns, issue dependencies, workload balancing, and reporting checkpoints.

prompt_templates.title: Design Cross-Repository Coordination
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a cross-repository GitHub coordination plan using swarm agents. Include dependency mapping, synchronization rules, approval gates, and risks from automation.

output_examples.input: Create a coordinated issue for an authentication feature.
output_examples.output: A structured issue plan with a clear title, labels, acceptance criteria, swarm roles, subtasks, and review checkpoints.

output_examples.input: Analyze stale issues in a repository.
output: Suggested comments and labels for review

output_examples.input: Plan the next sprint from the project board.
output_examples.output: A sprint outline with prioritized issues, milestone targets, dependency risks, agent assignments, and progress metrics.

best_practices: Run proposed GitHub commands in review mode before allowing writes to issues or projects.
Use least-privilege GitHub tokens and require approval for webhooks or cross-organization sync.
Pin third-party actions and package versions before adopting automation in production workflows.
anti_patterns: Running bulk issue edits or stale closures without checking the selected issue set first.
Sending project reports to external channels without confirming data sensitivity and recipients.
Using broad repository permissions for automation that only needs issue or project access.

faq.question: Does this skill require GitHub CLI?
faq.answer: Yes. Many workflows use the GitHub CLI and require an authenticated account with repository permissions.

faq.question: Can it update live GitHub projects?
faq.answer: Yes. Several examples create, edit, label, comment on, or close GitHub issues and project items.

faq.question: Is this skill safe for private repositories?
faq.answer: Use it with caution. Review commands, restrict tokens, and approve any webhook or external reporting destination.

faq.question: Does it include runnable scripts?
faq.answer: The skill is a Markdown guide with command examples. Users must decide which commands to run.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, and Claude Code use it?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: What should teams review before publishing automation?
faq.answer: Review command scope, package pinning, GitHub token permissions, issue selection rules, and external data destinations.
